page: Amazon Web Services i2.4xlarge is a storage-optimized second-generation server instance. It is powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture. This instance features 16 dedicated vCPUs derived from 8 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled, operating at a clock speed of 2.5 GHz. The CPU configuration includes a 25 MB L3 cache and supports AVX and AES instruction sets, though it lacks newer extensions like AVX-512 or AMX. The system is equipped with 122.0 GB of system memory, providing 7.62 GB of RAM per core, and runs on the Xen hypervisor.

A defining characteristic of the i2.4xlarge is its bundled local storage, consisting of 3200 GB of NVMe SSD capacity. This high-capacity local storage is designed for applications requiring rapid access to large datasets. For external connectivity, the instance provides a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps. It does not include any complimentary public IPv4 addresses or GPU hardware.

Performance benchmarks indicate a mix of average and weak capabilities, reflecting its older hardware generation. The instance achieves average ratings in stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU tests, as well as in PassMark CPU and memory evaluations. However, single-core processing is a bottleneck, with Geekbench single-core scores rated as poor and text processing performance also scoring poor. Memory write bandwidth for large blocks is weak at 18.74 GB/sec, and compression tasks like single-threaded gzip and bzip3 decompression show weak performance. Conversely, database operations perform at an average level, with Redis SET operations reaching 6.04 million operations per second and PassMark database operations scoring 6516.2.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the i2.4xlarge offers high local storage density and substantial memory capacity relative to its compute resources, making it a viable option for legacy storage-heavy workloads without requiring expensive modern compute architectures. However, the tradeoff is lower CPU performance per watt and weaker single-threaded execution compared to newer generations. This instance is best suited for database hosting, NoSQL data stores, transactional databases, and static web serving, where high local NVMe storage capacity and moderate memory are prioritized over raw computational speed or advanced machine learning inference.
description: Amazon Web Services i2.4xlarge is a storage-optimized, second-generation instance designed for data-intensive workloads. Powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor with 16 dedicated vCPUs and 122.0 GB of RAM, it features a substantial 3200 GB of local NVMe SSD storage and a 2 Gbps baseline network bandwidth. Running on the Xen hypervisor, its performance profile is characterized by average multi-core CPU and database execution, alongside weaker single-core and compression performance. Benchmark results show average database operations and Redis SET throughput, but poor Geekbench single-core scores. This hardware profile makes the i2.4xlarge a cost-effective choice for legacy database hosting, NoSQL data stores, and static web serving where high-capacity local storage and moderate memory density are required, though it presents tradeoffs in raw computational efficiency and single-threaded processing speeds.
og_description: Amazon Web Services i2.4xlarge is a storage-optimized instance featuring 16 vCPUs, 122 GB RAM, and 3200 GB of local NVMe SSD storage, suitable for database and high-capacity local storage workloads.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services i2.4xlarge is a storage-optimized instance with 16 vCPUs, 122 GB RAM, and 3200 GB NVMe SSD storage for database workloads.
tagline: A storage-optimized instance featuring high-capacity local NVMe SSDs and balanced memory for data-intensive legacy database workloads.
bullet_points: 16 dedicated vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor
122.0 GB of system memory providing 7.62 GB per core
3200 GB of high-speed local NVMe SSD storage
2 Gbps baseline network bandwidth on the Xen hypervisor
Best suited for legacy databases, NoSQL stores, and static web serving
